title: GCD deletion
description: Max gcd after removing one element

examples.explanation: Array: [4,8,6,12]. Try removing index 0 (4): remaining [8,6,12]. Compute GCD: gcd(8,6)=2, gcd(2,12)=2. Try removing index 1 (8): remaining [4,6,12]. gcd(4,6)=2, gcd(2,12)=2. Try removing index 2 (6): remaining [4,8,12]. gcd(4,8)=4, gcd(4,12)=4. Try removing index 3 (12): remaining [4,8,6]. gcd(4,8)=4, gcd(4,6)=2. Maximum is 4.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² log m)
approaches.summary: For each element removed, compute GCD of remaining elements by iterating through the array. This involves nested loops where each GCD computation takes O(log m) time for numbers up to m.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum GCD After Removing One Element
ladders.desc: Given an array of positive integers, find the maximum possible GCD after removing exactly one element.
ladders.explanation: The brute force O(n² log m) approach solves this by systematically testing every possible element removal. For each element we consider removing, we compute the GCD of all remaining elements. The maximum GCD across all these scenarios is our answer. Intuition: Since we must remove exactly one element, the solution space is limited to n possibilities. For each possibility, we need to compute the GCD of n-1 elements. The naive way is to recompute GCD from scratch each time, leading to O(n² log m) complexity. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize max_gcd = 0. 2. For i from 0 to n-1 (element to remove): a. Initialize current_gcd = 0. b. For j from 0 to n-1: if j ≠ i: if current_gcd == 0: current_gcd = arr[j] else: current_gcd = gcd(current_gcd, arr[j]). c. Update max_gcd = max(max_gcd, current_gcd). 3. Return max_gcd. Time: O(n² log m) where m is max array value. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the GCD computation subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty arrays (n=1), not initializing GCD properly, or using inefficient GCD implementation. Real pattern: nested loops with GCD computation.

examples.trace: Array: [4,8,6,12]. Try removing index 0 (4): remaining [8,6,12]. Compute GCD: gcd(8,6)=2, gcd(2,12)=2. Try removing index 1 (8): remaining [4,6,12]. gcd(4,6)=2, gcd(2,12)=2. Try removing index 2 (6): remaining [4,8,12]. gcd(4,8)=4, gcd(4,12)=4. Try removing index 3 (12): remaining [4,8,6]. gcd(4,8)=4, gcd(4,6)=2. Maximum is 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute GCD of All Elements Except One
ladders.desc: Given an array and an index to exclude, compute the GCD of all other elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the core computation needed for each candidate removal in the main problem. Intuition: To find the GCD of n-1 elements, we can iterate through the array, skipping the excluded index, and accumulate GCD. The GCD operation is associative, so we can compute it sequentially. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize result = 0. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: if i ≠ exclude_index: if result == 0: result = arr[i] else: result = gcd(result, arr[i]). 3. Return result. Time: O(n log m). Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called n times in the full solution. Connection to L2: This relies on the GCD operation. Common mistakes: Not handling the first element properly (when result is 0), or incorrect index comparison. Real pattern: loop with conditional and GCD accumulation.

examples.trace: Start result=0. Index 0 (4): not excluded, result=0 so set to 4. Index 1 (8): not excluded, gcd(4,8)=4. Index 2 (6): excluded, skip. Index 3 (12): not excluded, gcd(4,12)=4. Final result=4.
Start result=0. Index 0: excluded, skip. Index 1 (9): result=0 so set to 9. Index 2 (15): gcd(9,15)=3. Index 3 (27): gcd(3,27)=3. Final result=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative GCD Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the GCD of two numbers using the Euclidean algorithm iteratively.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm is based on the property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Intuition: If we repeatedly replace the larger number with the remainder when divided by the smaller number, we eventually reach a remainder of 0, at which point the other number is the GCD. This works because the GCD divides both numbers and any common divisor must also divide the remainder. Step-by-step: 1. While b ≠ 0: temp = b, b = a mod b, a = temp. 2. Return a. Time: O(log min(a,b)). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the gcd function used in the subroutine. Connection to L3: This builds on the modulo operation. Common mistakes: Not handling negative numbers (but our problem has positives), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly. Real pattern: while loop with modulo and swap.

examples.trace: Start: a=48, b=18. Iteration 1: b=18≠0, temp=18, b=48 mod 18=12, a=18. Iteration 2: b=12≠0, temp=12, b=18 mod 12=6, a=12. Iteration 3: b=6≠0, temp=6, b=12 mod 6=0, a=6. b=0, return a=6.
Start: a=27, b=15. Iteration 1: b=15≠0, temp=15, b=27 mod 15=12, a=15. Iteration 2: b=12≠0, temp=12, b=15 mod 12=3, a=12. Iteration 3: b=3≠0, temp=3, b=12 mod 3=0, a=3. b=0, return a=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation (a % b) returns the remainder when a is divided by b. Intuition: For positive integers, a = b * q + r where 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo gives r. This is fundamental to the Euclidean algorithm because it reduces the problem size. Step-by-step: For a ≥ 0 and b > 0, compute r = a - b * (a // b). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is used in each iteration of the GCD algorithm. Connection to L4: This relies on integer division. Common mistakes: Confusing with integer division, not handling b=0 (undefined). Real pattern: basic arithmetic operation.

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3 (17//5=3), remainder = 17 - 5*3 = 17-15=2.
48 divided by 18 gives quotient 2 (48//18=2), remainder = 48 - 18*2 = 48-36=12.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division
ladders.desc: Divide two integers and return the quotient as an integer.
ladders.explanation: Integer division (a // b) returns the floor of the division result for positive integers. Intuition: It tells how many times b fits completely into a. For example, 17 // 5 = 3 because 5 fits into 17 three times (with remainder). This is used in the modulo computation. Step-by-step: For a ≥ 0 and b > 0, compute q = floor(a / b). In programming, this is typically the // operator. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is needed to compute the modulo. Connection to L5: This builds on basic arithmetic. Common mistakes: Confusing with float division, not handling division by zero. Real pattern: basic arithmetic.

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5: 5*3=15 ≤ 17, 5*4=20 > 17, so quotient=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what GCD means: the largest positive integer that divides two numbers without remainder.
ladders.explanation: The GCD of two integers a and b is the largest integer d such that d divides both a and b. Intuition: It represents the greatest shared factor. For example, GCD(12,18)=6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. Key properties: GCD(a,0)=a, GCD(a,b)=GCD(b,a), and if a divides b, GCD(a,b)=a. This is the foundational concept for the entire problem. Step-by-step to find manually: List factors of each number and find the largest common one. Time: O(min(a,b)) for naive method. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: GCD computation uses division. Common mistakes: Confusing with LCM, not considering 1 as always a divisor. Real pattern: conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Factors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Factors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Common factors: 1,2,3,6. Largest is 6.
Factors of 7: 1,7. Factors of 13: 1,13. Common factor: 1. Largest is 1.

approaches.name: Prefix and Suffix GCD Arrays O(n log m)
approaches.summary: Precompute prefix GCDs from left to right and suffix GCDs from right to left. For each element at index i, compute GCD(prefix[i-1], suffix[i+1]) to get the GCD after removing that element, in O(1) time per element.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum GCD After Removing One Element
ladders.desc: Given an array, find the maximum GCD after removing exactly one element.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the maximum possible GCD after removing exactly one element from an array. A naive approach would try removing each element and recomputing the GCD of the remaining elements, which would be O(n² log m) time. The optimal approach uses prefix and suffix GCD arrays to achieve O(n log m) time. The intuition is that when we remove element at index i, the GCD of the remaining elements equals GCD(prefix[i-1], suffix[i+1]), where prefix[i] stores GCD of first i+1 elements and suffix[i] stores GCD of last n-i elements. We precompute these arrays in O(n log m) time, then for each possible removal, compute the candidate GCD in O(1) time, tracking the maximum. The algorithm: 1. Handle edge cases (n=1 returns 0, n=2 returns max of the two elements). 2. Create prefix array: prefix[0]=arr[0], then prefix[i]=GCD(prefix[i-1], arr[i]). 3. Create suffix array: suffix[n-1]=arr[n-1], then suffix[i]=GCD(suffix[i+1], arr[i]). 4. For each i from 0 to n-1: if i=0, candidate=suffix[1]; if i=n-1, candidate=prefix[n-2]; else candidate=GCD(prefix[i-1], suffix[i+1]). 5. Return max of all candidates. Time: O(n log m) where m is max array value (GCD computation is O(log m)). Space: O(n) for the two arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the GCD computation subroutine extensively. Common mistakes: Not handling n=1 or n=2 correctly, off-by-one errors in prefix/suffix indices, forgetting that GCD(0,x)=x. Real code pattern: def max_gcd_after_removal(arr): n=len(arr); if n==1: return 0; if n==2: return max(arr); prefix=[0]*n; suffix=[0]*n; prefix[0]=arr[0]; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i]=gcd(prefix[i-1],arr[i]); suffix[n-1]=arr[n-1]; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): suffix[i]=gcd(suffix[i+1],arr[i]); ans=0; for i in range(n): if i==0: cand=suffix[1]; elif i==n-1: cand=prefix[n-2]; else: cand=gcd(prefix[i-1],suffix[i+1]); ans=max(ans,cand); return ans.

examples.trace: Prefix=[1,1,1,1,1], suffix=[1,1,1,1,5]. Candidates for all removals are 1, so max=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing GCD Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the Euclidean algorithm to compute GCD of two numbers.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm is a fundamental method for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers. It is based on the principle that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). This algorithm is efficient with O(log min(a,b)) time complexity. The intuition is that if d divides both a and b, then it also divides a-b, and by extension a mod b. By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the numbers until one becomes zero, at which point the other is the GCD. Step-by-step: 1. While b ≠ 0: set a, b = b, a % b. 2. Return a. For non-positive numbers, we typically take absolute values. Time analysis: Each iteration reduces the numbers significantly; in the worst case (consecutive Fibonacci numbers), it takes O(log min(a,b)) steps. Space: O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in computing prefix and suffix GCD arrays and in the candidate GCD computations. Common mistakes: Not handling zero correctly (GCD(a,0)=|a|), infinite loop if not updating variables properly, integer overflow in languages with fixed-size integers (but Python handles big integers). Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a,b = b, a%b; return abs(a). For the prefix/suffix computation, we apply this sequentially to array elements.

examples.trace: Start: a=48, b=18. Since b≠0: compute a mod b = 48 % 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12. Now b≠0: 18 % 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6. b≠0: 12 % 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0. Now b=0, so return a=6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix and Suffix GCD Arrays
ladders.desc: Precompute GCD of prefixes and suffixes to answer range GCD queries in O(1).
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that to compute the GCD of an array after removing one element, we can use precomputed prefix and suffix GCD arrays. Prefix[i] stores GCD of elements from index 0 to i. Suffix[i] stores GCD of elements from index i to n-1. Then, after removing element at index i, the GCD of remaining elements is GCD(prefix[i-1], suffix[i+1]) (with appropriate boundary handling). This works because GCD is associative: GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(GCD(a,b), c). So the GCD of the left part (0 to i-1) is prefix[i-1], and the right part (i+1 to n-1) is suffix[i+1], and the overall GCD is GCD of these two. The intuition is similar to prefix sums but for GCD. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize prefix[0] = arr[0]. For i from 1 to n-1: prefix[i] = GCD(prefix[i-1], arr[i]). 2. Initialize suffix[n-1] = arr[n-1]. For i from n-2 down to 0: suffix[i] = GCD(suffix[i+1], arr[i]). Time: O(n log m) for computing both arrays, as each GCD call is O(log m). Space: O(n) for the two arrays. Connection to L0: This is the main technique that enables O(n) candidate computations after O(n) preprocessing. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, not handling n=1 or n=2 separately, using wrong direction for suffix computation. Real code pattern: prefix=[0]*n; prefix[0]=arr[0]; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i]=gcd(prefix[i-1],arr[i]); suffix=[0]*n; suffix[n-1]=arr[n-1]; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): suffix[i]=gcd(suffix[i+1],arr[i]).

examples.trace: Array: [4,8,12,16]. Prefix: start prefix[0]=4. i=1: prefix[1]=GCD(4,8)=4. i=2: prefix[2]=GCD(4,12)=4. i=3: prefix[3]=GCD(4,16)=4. So prefix: [4,4,4,4]. Suffix: start suffix[3]=16. i=2: suffix[2]=GCD(16,12)=4. i=1: suffix[1]=GCD(4,8)=4. i=0: suffix[0]=GCD(4,4)=4. So suffix: [4,4,4,16].

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing GCD of an Array
ladders.desc: Compute the GCD of all elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-problem involves finding the GCD of multiple numbers. The GCD of an array is computed iteratively: start with the first element as the current GCD, then for each subsequent element, update the current GCD to GCD(current_gcd, element). This works because GCD is associative and commutative: GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(GCD(a,b), c). The intuition is that the GCD of the entire set must divide each element, so we can accumulate it pairwise. Step-by-step: 1. If array is empty, return 0 (or handle as per problem). 2. Initialize result = arr[0]. 3. For each element in arr[1:]: result = GCD(result, element). 4. Return result. Time: O(n log m) where n is array length and m is max value, since each GCD call is O(log m). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is used to compute each prefix and suffix value in the arrays. Common mistakes: Not handling zero correctly (GCD(0,x)=|x|), assuming GCD of empty array is 0 or 1, integer overflow. Real code pattern: def gcd_array(arr): if not arr: return 0; res=arr[0]; for num in arr[1:]: res=gcd(res,num); return res.

examples.trace: Start: res=12. Next element 18: GCD(12,18)=6. Update res=6. Next element 24: GCD(6,24)=6. Final res=6.
Start: res=7. Next 14: GCD(7,14)=7. Next 21: GCD(7,21)=7. Next 28: GCD(7,28)=7. Final 7.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted a % b, returns the remainder when a is divided by b. It is fundamental to the Euclidean algorithm. For positive integers, a % b is in range [0, b-1]. If b=0, it's typically an error. The intuition: a = b * q + r, where q is quotient and r is remainder with 0 ≤ r < |b|. In Python, % always returns a non-negative remainder. Step-by-step: To compute a % b, subtract b from a repeatedly until result is less than b, but in practice use built-in operator. Time: O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in each step of the Euclidean algorithm to reduce numbers. Common mistakes: Confusing modulo with integer division, not handling negative numbers correctly (different languages have different definitions), division by zero. Real code pattern: remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3, remainder 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2.
10 divided by 3 gives quotient 3, remainder 1 because 10 = 3*3 + 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what GCD is and how to find it for two small numbers.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers without leaving a remainder. For example, GCD(12,18)=6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. Key properties: GCD(a,0)=|a|, GCD(a,b)=GCD(b,a), and if a and b are positive, GCD(a,b) ≤ min(a,b). To find GCD for small numbers, list all divisors of each number and find the largest common one. Step-by-step: 1. Find all positive divisors of a. 2. Find all positive divisors of b. 3. Identify common divisors. 4. Pick the largest. Time: O(min(a,b)) for naive divisor listing. Space: O(min(a,b)) to store divisors. Connection to L4: This foundational concept leads to the Euclidean algorithm which uses modulo to compute GCD efficiently. Common mistakes: Forgetting that GCD is always positive, confusing GCD with LCM (least common multiple), not handling zero correctly. Real code pattern: For small numbers, use brute force: def gcd_naive(a,b): if a==0: return abs(b); if b==0: return abs(a); limit = min(abs(a),abs(b)); for i in range(limit,0,-1): if a%i==0 and b%i==0: return i.

examples.trace: Divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Divisors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Common divisors: 1,2,3,6. Largest is 6.
Divisors of 7: 1,7. Divisors of 5: 1,5. Common divisor: 1. So GCD=1.

approaches.name: GCD of GCDs Optimization O(n log m)
approaches.summary: Compute overall GCD of the array, then for each element, compute the GCD after removal as GCD(overall_gcd, element) using the property that removing an element affects the GCD based on its contribution. This leverages mathematical properties of GCD.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum GCD After Removing One Element
ladders.desc: Given an array of positive integers, find the maximum possible GCD after removing exactly one element.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum GCD achievable by removing exactly one element from the array. The naive approach would be O(n²) by recomputing GCD for each removal. The optimized approach leverages mathematical properties of GCD: for an array A, let G = gcd of all elements. When removing element A[i], the new GCD becomes gcd(G, A[i]). This works because gcd(A[0],...,A[i-1],A[i+1],...,A[n-1]) = gcd(G, A[i]) where G = gcd of entire array. Intuition: The overall GCD G contains all common factors. Removing A[i] means we're essentially computing GCD of all elements except A[i], which equals GCD of (G, A[i]) since G already represents the common factors of all elements, and we need to exclude A[i]'s contribution. Step-by-step: 1. Compute overall GCD G of all elements. 2. For each element A[i], compute candidate = gcd(G, A[i]). 3. Track maximum candidate. 4. Return maximum. Time: O(n log m) where m is max element value (GCD computation cost). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of computing GCD after removal via gcd(G, A[i]). Common mistakes: Not handling edge cases like single element arrays (return 0 or handle specially), misunderstanding that we need maximum after removal not minimum, incorrect GCD computation for negative numbers (problem states positive integers). Real code pattern: def max_gcd_after_removal(arr): n = len(arr); if n == 1: return 0; overall_gcd = arr[0]; for i in range(1,n): overall_gcd = gcd(overall_gcd, arr[i]); max_val = 0; for i in range(n): candidate = gcd(overall_gcd, arr[i]); max_val = max(max_val, candidate); return max_val;

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute overall GCD G. Start with 4. gcd(4,8)=4. gcd(4,12)=4. gcd(4,16)=4. So G=4. Step 2: For each element compute gcd(G, element). For 4: gcd(4,4)=4. For 8: gcd(4,8)=4. For 12: gcd(4,12)=4. For 16: gcd(4,16)=4. Step 3: Maximum among {4,4,4,4}=4. Step 4: Return 4.
Prefix=[6,2,1], suffix=[1,5,15]. After removing 6 -> 5, removing 10 -> 3, removing 15 -> 2. Max=5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute GCD After Removing One Element Using Prefix and Suffix GCD
ladders.desc: Given an array and an index, compute the GCD of the array after removing that element using precomputed prefix and suffix GCD arrays.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the optimized solution. Instead of recomputing GCD from scratch for each removal (O(n²)), we precompute prefix_gcd and suffix_gcd arrays in O(n log m) time. Prefix_gcd[i] stores GCD of elements from index 0 to i. Suffix_gcd[i] stores GCD of elements from index i to n-1. Then, to compute GCD after removing element at index i, we combine prefix_gcd[i-1] and suffix_gcd[i+1] using GCD. Intuition: The GCD of all elements except A[i] is the GCD of the GCD of elements before i and the GCD of elements after i. This works because GCD is associative and commutative. Step-by-step: 1. Compute prefix_gcd: initialize prefix_gcd[0]=A[0]; for i from 1 to n-1: prefix_gcd[i]=gcd(prefix_gcd[i-1], A[i]). 2. Compute suffix_gcd: initialize suffix_gcd[n-1]=A[n-1]; for i from n-2 down to 0: suffix_gcd[i]=gcd(suffix_gcd[i+1], A[i]). 3. For a given index i, if i==0: candidate = suffix_gcd[1]; if i==n-1: candidate = prefix_gcd[n-2]; else: candidate = gcd(prefix_gcd[i-1], suffix_gcd[i+1]). Time: O(n log m) for precomputation, O(1) per query. Space: O(n) for storing arrays, but can be optimized to O(1) by computing on the fly. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called for each index to find the maximum. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in array indices, not handling single-element arrays, incorrect GCD function implementation. Real code pattern: def gcd_after_removal(arr, i, prefix, suffix): n = len(arr); if n == 1: return 0; if i == 0: return suffix[1]; if i == n-1: return prefix[n-2]; return gcd(prefix[i-1], suffix[i+1]);

examples.trace: Step 1: Precompute prefix_gcd. prefix[0]=6. prefix[1]=gcd(6,10)=2. prefix[2]=gcd(2,15)=1. Step 2: Precompute suffix_gcd. suffix[2]=15. suffix[1]=gcd(10,15)=5. suffix[0]=gcd(6,5)=1. Step 3: For index i=1, candidate = gcd(prefix[0], suffix[2]) = gcd(6,15)=3. Step 4: Return 3.
Using prefix=[6,2,1] and suffix=[1,5,15]: remove 6 -> 5, remove 10 -> 3, remove 15 -> 2. Maximum=5.

Step 1: Precompute prefix_gcd. prefix[0]=4. prefix[1]=gcd(4,8)=4. prefix[2]=gcd(4,12)=4. prefix[3]=gcd(4,16)=4. Step 2: Precompute suffix_gcd. suffix[3]=16. suffix[2]=gcd(12,16)=4. suffix[1]=gcd(8,4)=4. suffix[0]=gcd(4,4)=4. Step 3: For index i=2, candidate = gcd(prefix[1], suffix[3]) = gcd(4,16)=4. Step 4: Return 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Combining Prefix and Suffix GCD to Compute GCD of Subarray Excluding One Element
ladders.desc: Understand how to compute GCD of an array excluding one element by combining GCD of prefix and suffix segments.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that for an array A, the GCD of all elements except A[i] can be computed as gcd(prefix_gcd[i-1], suffix_gcd[i+1]), where prefix_gcd[i-1] is GCD of elements before i, and suffix_gcd[i+1] is GCD of elements after i. Intuition: GCD is an associative operation, meaning gcd(a,b,c) = gcd(gcd(a,b), c). When we remove A[i], we're essentially taking the GCD of two independent segments: the segment before i and the segment after i. Since these segments are disjoint, their combined GCD is the GCD of their individual GCDs. This avoids recomputing over overlapping elements. Step-by-step: Given prefix array P where P[j] = gcd(A[0..j]), and suffix array S where S[j] = gcd(A[j..n-1]). For index i, the GCD after removal is: if i=0: S[1] (GCD of elements from index 1 to end); if i=n-1: P[n-2] (GCD of elements from start to n-2); else: gcd(P[i-1], S[i+1]). This works because P[i-1] captures all common factors of elements before i, and S[i+1] captures all common factors of elements after i. Their GCD gives the common factors of the entire array excluding A[i]. Time: O(1) per index after precomputation. Space: O(n) for storing P and S. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the sub-routine. Common mistakes: Assuming this works without precomputation, not handling edge cases where i is at boundaries, misunderstanding associativity of GCD. Real code pattern: def compute_gcd_excluding(arr, i): # Assume prefix and suffix precomputed; n = len(arr); if n == 1: return 0; if i == 0: return suffix[1]; if i == n-1: return prefix[n-2]; return gcd(prefix[i-1], suffix[i+1]);

examples.trace: Step 1: Given prefix array P = [6,2,1] for array [6,10,15]. P[0]=6 (gcd of first element), P[1]=2 (gcd of first two), P[2]=1 (gcd of all three). Step 2: Given suffix array S = [1,5,15]. S[0]=1 (gcd of all), S[1]=5 (gcd of last two), S[2]=15 (gcd of last element). Step 3: For index i=1, compute gcd(P[i-1], S[i+1]) = gcd(P[0], S[2]) = gcd(6,15)=3. Step 4: This represents GCD of array after removing element at index 1 (value 10), which is gcd(6,15)=3.

Step 1: Prefix P = [4,4,4,4] for array [4,8,12,16]. Step 2: Suffix S = [4,4,4,4]. Step 3: For index i=2, compute gcd(P[1], S[3]) = gcd(4,4)=4. Step 4: This is GCD after removing element at index 2 (value 12), which is gcd(4,8,16)=4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Precomputing Prefix and Suffix GCD Arrays
ladders.desc: Compute arrays where each element stores the GCD of all elements up to that index (prefix) and from that index to the end (suffix).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating two auxiliary arrays: prefix_gcd and suffix_gcd. Prefix_gcd[i] holds the GCD of the subarray from index 0 to i. Suffix_gcd[i] holds the GCD of the subarray from index i to n-1. These arrays enable O(1) queries for GCD of any contiguous subarray or, as used here, for combining to exclude an element. Intuition: By computing GCD progressively, we avoid redundant calculations. For prefix, we start with the first element and iteratively combine with next elements using GCD. For suffix, we start from the end and move backwards. This leverages the associative property of GCD: gcd(a,b,c) = gcd(gcd(a,b), c). Step-by-step: For prefix: initialize prefix[0] = A[0]; for i from 1 to n-1: prefix[i] = gcd(prefix[i-1], A[i]). For suffix: initialize suffix[n-1] = A[n-1]; for i from n-2 down to 0: suffix[i] = gcd(suffix[i+1], A[i]). Time: O(n log m) since each GCD operation takes O(log m) time. Space: O(n) for storing the arrays. Connection to L2: These arrays are used to quickly compute GCD after removal. Common mistakes: Incorrect iteration direction for suffix, not initializing properly, using wrong indices. Real code pattern: def precompute_gcd_arrays(arr): n = len(arr); prefix = [0]*n; suffix = [0]*n; prefix[0] = arr[0]; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i] = gcd(prefix[i-1], arr[i]); suffix[n-1] = arr[n-1]; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): suffix[i] = gcd(suffix[i+1], arr[i]); return prefix, suffix;

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute prefix. Start with prefix[0]=6. For i=1: prefix[1]=gcd(prefix[0]=6, arr[1]=10)=2. For i=2: prefix[2]=gcd(prefix[1]=2, arr[2]=15)=1. So prefix = [6,2,1]. Step 2: Compute suffix. Start with suffix[2]=15. For i=1: suffix[1]=gcd(suffix[2]=15, arr[1]=10)=5. For i=0: suffix[0]=gcd(suffix[1]=5, arr[0]=6)=1. So suffix = [1,5,15].

Step 1: Compute prefix. prefix[0]=4. prefix[1]=gcd(4,8)=4. prefix[2]=gcd(4,12)=4. prefix[3]=gcd(4,16)=4. So prefix = [4,4,4,4]. Step 2: Compute suffix. suffix[3]=16. suffix[2]=gcd(16,12)=4. suffix[1]=gcd(4,8)=4. suffix[0]=gcd(4,4)=4. So suffix = [4,4,4,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing GCD of Two Numbers Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the Euclidean algorithm to compute the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two positive integers.
ladders.explanation: The GCD of two numbers is the largest positive integer that divides both without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is an efficient method based on the principle that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Intuition: If a > b, then any common divisor of a and b also divides a - b. Repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder when divided by the smaller reduces the numbers until one becomes zero, at which point the other is the GCD. Step-by-step: Given a and b (positive integers). 1. While b != 0: temp = b; b = a mod b; a = temp. 2. Return a. Alternatively, using recursion: gcd(a,b) = b if a==0 else gcd(b%a, a). Time: O(log min(a,b)) because the numbers reduce quickly. Space: O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in precomputing prefix and suffix arrays. Common mistakes: Not handling zero correctly (gcd(0,x)=x), infinite loops if not updating variables properly, using subtraction instead of modulo (less efficient). Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return a;

examples.trace: Step 1: a=56, b=98. Since b!=0, compute: temp = b = 98; b = a mod b = 56 mod 98 = 56; a = temp = 98. Now a=98, b=56. Step 2: b!=0, compute: temp = 56; b = 98 mod 56 = 42; a = 56. Now a=56, b=42. Step 3: b!=0, compute: temp = 42; b = 56 mod 42 = 14; a = 42. Now a=42, b=14. Step 4: b!=0, compute: temp = 14; b = 42 mod 14 = 0; a = 14. Now a=14, b=0. Step 5: b==0, return a=14. So gcd(56,98)=14.

Step 1: a=48, b=18. b!=0: temp=18; b=48 mod 18=12; a=18. Now a=18, b=12. Step 2: b!=0: temp=12; b=18 mod 12=6; a=12. Now a=12, b=6. Step 3: b!=0: temp=6; b=12 mod 6=0; a=6. Now a=6, b=0. Step 4: b==0, return a=6. So gcd(48,18)=6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Learn what GCD is and how to compute it for small numbers by listing divisors.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two or more integers is the largest positive integer that divides each of the numbers without leaving a remainder. For example, GCD of 12 and 18 is 6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. Intuition: Divisors of a number are integers that divide it evenly. The GCD is the largest number that appears in the list of divisors for all given numbers. Step-by-step for two numbers a and b: 1. List all positive divisors of a. 2. List all positive divisors of b. 3. Find common divisors in both lists. 4. The largest common divisor is the GCD. For more than two numbers, you can compute pairwise. Time: O(min(a,b)) for naive listing, but this is for understanding only. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This foundational concept leads to the efficient Euclidean algorithm. Common mistakes: Confusing GCD with LCM (least common multiple), forgetting 1 is always a divisor, not considering negative numbers (but we deal with positives). Real code pattern for naive approach: def gcd_naive(a,b): gcd_val = 1; for i in range(1, min(a,b)+1): if a%i==0 and b%i==0: gcd_val = i; return gcd_val;

examples.trace: Step 1: Divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Step 2: Divisors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Step 3: Common divisors: 1,2,3,6. Step 4: Largest common divisor: 6. So GCD(12,18)=6.
Step 1: Divisors of 8: 1,2,4,8. Step 2: Divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Step 3: Common divisors: 1,2,4. Step 4: Largest common divisor: 4. So GCD(8,12)=4.
